reasoning: The informal statement "The only polynomials P for which [equation] are the constant polynomials 2+√5 and 2-√5" asserts a complete characterization: the solution set equals exactly {C(2+√5), C(2-√5)}. This is a biconditional claim: (a) every solution must be one of these two constants, AND (b) these two constants actually are solutions. The Lean signature only captures direction (a): assuming P satisfies the equation, it concludes P equals one of the two constants. It does not assert that these constants actually do satisfy the functional equation — the Lean statement would remain true even if only one (or neither) of them were a solution, since the conclusion is a disjunction. Thus the Lean statement is logically implied by the informal one (it captures the uniqueness/classification direction) but does not imply the full informal claim (the verification direction is missing), making it strictly weaker.
